title: Test statistic in a two-sample z test for the difference of proportions
description: Calculate the test statistic in a two-sample z test for the difference of proportions.
topic_path: Testing for the difference of two population proportions
Test statistic in a two-sample z test for the difference of proportions

assessment_items.question_content:
A large school district was considering changing the start times to its schools. The change would involve high schools starting later and elementary schools starting earlier. The school district was curious if parents supported this change, so they surveyed separate random samples of parents from both types of school in the district. Here are their results:

Supports change? | High school | Elementary
- | -: | -:
Yes | $54$ | $24$
No | $46$ | $26$
**Total** | $100$ | $50$

The school district wants to use these results to carry out a two-sample $z$ test to determine if the proportion of parents who support the change is significantly different for each type of school. Assume that all conditions have been met.

**Which of the following would be an appropriate test statistic for the district's test?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Pooling the proportion of successes

In this type of test, it's useful to first calculate the pooled (or combined) proportion of successes in both samples:

$\hat p_\text{C}=\dfrac{\text{combined count of successes}}{\text{combined sample sizes}}$

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###Formula for test statistic

The test statistic for this test is:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\text{sample difference}-\text{hypothesized difference}}{\text{standard error of the difference}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\left(\hat p_1-\hat p_2\right)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\hat p_\text{C}(1-\hat p_\text{C})}{n_1}+\dfrac{\hat p_\text{C}(1-\hat p_\text{C})}{n_2}}}
\end{align}$

###Components of formula

Type of school | High school | Elementary
- | -: | -:
Supports change | $\blueD{54}$ | $\greenD{24}$
Doesn't support | $46$ | $26$
**Total** | $\purpleD{100}$ | $\goldD{50}$

$\begin{align}
\hat p_\text{C}&=\dfrac{\blueD{54}+\greenD{24}}{\purpleD{100}+\goldD{50}}=0.52 \\\\
\hat p_\text{H.S.}&=\dfrac{\blueD{54}}{\purpleD{100}}=0.54 \\\\
\hat p_\text{Elem.}&=\dfrac{\greenD{24}}{\goldD{50}}=0.48
\end{align}$

Plugging these values in, our test statistic is:

$z=\dfrac{(0.54-0.48)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.52(0.48)}{100}+\dfrac{0.52(0.48)}{50}}}$

###Answer

An appropriate test statistic would be:

$z=\dfrac{(0.54-0.48)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.52(0.48)}{100}+\dfrac{0.52(0.48)}{50}}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Researchers observed a random sample of drivers and recorded data about their phone usage while driving. The researchers saw $160$ of the $800$ women in the sample using phones, while $300$ of the $1200$ men were seen using phones.

The researchers want to test if these results suggest a significant difference between the proportions of men and women that use their phones while driving. Assume that all conditions have been met.

**Which of the following would be an appropriate test statistic for their test?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Pooling the proportion of successes

In this type of test, it's useful to first calculate the pooled (or combined) proportion of successes in both samples:

$\hat p_\text{C}=\dfrac{\text{combined count of successes}}{\text{combined sample sizes}}$

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###Formula for test statistic

The test statistic for this test is:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\text{sample difference}-\text{hypothesized difference}}{\text{standard error of the difference}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\left(\hat p_1-\hat p_2\right)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\hat p_\text{C}(1-\hat p_\text{C})}{n_1}+\dfrac{\hat p_\text{C}(1-\hat p_\text{C})}{n_2}}}
\end{align}$

###Components of formula

The researchers saw $\greenD{160}$ of the $\goldD{800}$ women in the sample using phones, while $\blueD{300}$ of the $\purpleD{1200}$ men were seen using phones.

$\begin{align}
\hat p_\text{C}&=\dfrac{\blueD{300}+\greenD{160}}{\purpleD{1200}+\goldD{800}}=0.23 \\\\
\hat p_\text{M}&=\dfrac{\blueD{300}}{\purpleD{1200}}=0.25 \\\\
\hat p_\text{W}&=\dfrac{\greenD{160}}{\goldD{800}}=0.20
\end{align}$

Plugging these values in, our test statistic is:

$z=\dfrac{(0.25-0.20)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.23(0.77)}{1200}+\dfrac{0.23(0.77)}{800}}}$

###Answer

An appropriate test statistic would be:

$z=\dfrac{(0.25-0.20)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.23(0.77)}{1200}+\dfrac{0.23(0.77)}{800}}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Market researchers wondered if teens were more or less likely than adults to use their mobile phone as an alarm clock. The researchers surveyed a random sample of people and removed those who didn't have a mobile phone. Here are the results:

Use phone as alarm? | Teens | Adults
- | -: | -:
Yes | $70$ | $150$
No | $30$ | $150$
**Total** | $100$ | $300$

The researchers want to test if these results suggest a significant difference between the proportions of teens and adults that use their phone as an alarm clock. Assume that all conditions have been met.

**Which of the following would be an appropriate test statistic for their test?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Pooling the proportion of successes

In this type of test, it's useful to first calculate the pooled (or combined) proportion of successes in both samples:

$\hat p_\text{C}=\dfrac{\text{combined count of successes}}{\text{combined sample sizes}}$

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###Formula for test statistic

The test statistic for this test is:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\text{sample difference}-\text{hypothesized difference}}{\text{standard error of the difference}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\left(\hat p_1-\hat p_2\right)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\hat p_\text{C}(1-\hat p_\text{C})}{n_1}+\dfrac{\hat p_\text{C}(1-\hat p_\text{C})}{n_2}}}
\end{align}$

###Components of formula

Use phone as alarm? | Teens | Adults
- | -: | -:
Yes | $\blueD{70}$ | $\greenD{150}$
No | $30$ | $150$
**Total** | $\purpleD{100}$ | $\goldD{300}$

$\begin{align}
\hat p_\text{C}&=\dfrac{\blueD{70}+\greenD{150}}{\purpleD{100}+\goldD{300}}=0.55 \\\\
\hat p_\text{T}&=\dfrac{\blueD{70}}{\purpleD{100}}=0.7 \\\\
\hat p_\text{A}&=\dfrac{\greenD{150}}{\goldD{300}}=0.5
\end{align}$

Plugging these values in, our test statistic is:

$z=\dfrac{(0.7-0.5)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.55(0.45)}{100}+\dfrac{0.55(0.45)}{300}}}$

###Answer

An appropriate test statistic would be:

$z=\dfrac{(0.7-0.5)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.55(0.45)}{100}+\dfrac{0.55(0.45)}{300}}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Joshua plays basketball, and he learned a new technique for shooting free throws. He decided to shoot $50$ free throws with his old technique and $50$ with the new technique to see if there was a difference in his performance. He made $30$ of $50$ free throws using his old technique, and he made $38$ of $50$ using the new technique. He took these shots over multiple days and alternated techniques, so he's willing to assume his data satisfy the conditions for inference.

Joshua wants to test if these results suggest a significant difference between the proportions of free throws made with each technique.

**Which of the following would be an appropriate test statistic for their test?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Pooling the proportion of successes

In this type of test, it's useful to first calculate the pooled (or combined) proportion of successes in both samples:

$\hat p_\text{C}=\dfrac{\text{combined count of successes}}{\text{combined sample sizes}}$

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###Formula for test statistic

The test statistic for this test is:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\text{sample difference}-\text{hypothesized difference}}{\text{standard error of the difference}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\left(\hat p_1-\hat p_2\right)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\hat p_\text{C}(1-\hat p_\text{C})}{n_1}+\dfrac{\hat p_\text{C}(1-\hat p_\text{C})}{n_2}}}
\end{align}$

###Components of formula

He made $\greenD{30}$ of $\goldD{50}$ free throws using his old technique, and he made $\blueD{38}$ of $\purpleD{50}$ using the new technique.

$\begin{align}
\hat p_\text{C}&=\dfrac{\blueD{38}+\greenD{30}}{\purpleD{50}+\goldD{50}}=0.68 \\\\
\hat p_\text{New}&=\dfrac{\blueD{38}}{\purpleD{50}}=0.76 \\\\
\hat p_\text{Old}&=\dfrac{\greenD{30}}{\goldD{50}}=0.60
\end{align}$

Plugging these values in, our test statistic is:

$z=\dfrac{(0.76-0.60)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.68(0.32)}{50}+\dfrac{0.68(0.32)}{50}}}$

###Answer

An appropriate test statistic would be:

$z=\dfrac{(0.76-0.60)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.68(0.32)}{50}+\dfrac{0.68(0.32)}{50}}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
A political consultant wondered if support for a candidate was significantly different between men and women. The consultant surveyed a random sample of voters. Here are the results:

Supports candidate? | Men | Women
- | -: | -:
Yes | $24$ | $48$
No | $56$ | $52$
**Total** | $80$ | $100$

The consultant wants to test if these results suggest a significant difference in support between men and women. Assume that all conditions have been met.

**Which of the following would be an appropriate test statistic for their test?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
###Pooling the proportion of successes

In this type of test, it's useful to first calculate the pooled (or combined) proportion of successes in both samples:

$\hat p_\text{C}=\dfrac{\text{combined count of successes}}{\text{combined sample sizes}}$

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###Formula for test statistic

The test statistic for this test is:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\text{sample difference}-\text{hypothesized difference}}{\text{standard error of the difference}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\left(\hat p_1-\hat p_2\right)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\hat p_\text{C}(1-\hat p_\text{C})}{n_1}+\dfrac{\hat p_\text{C}(1-\hat p_\text{C})}{n_2}}}
\end{align}$

###Components of formula

Supports candidate? | Male | Female
- | -: | -:
Yes | $\blueD{24}$ | $\greenD{48}$
No | $56$ | $52$
**Total** | $\purpleD{80}$ | $\goldD{100}$

$\begin{align}
\hat p_\text{C}&=\dfrac{\blueD{24}+\greenD{48}}{\purpleD{80}+\goldD{100}}=0.4 \\\\
\hat p_\text{M}&=\dfrac{\blueD{24}}{\purpleD{80}}=0.3 \\\\
\hat p_\text{F}&=\dfrac{\greenD{48}}{\goldD{100}}=0.48
\end{align}$

Plugging these values in, our test statistic is:

$z=\dfrac{(0.48-0.3)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.4(0.6)}{100}+\dfrac{0.4(0.6)}{80}}}$

###Answer

An appropriate test statistic would be:

$z=\dfrac{(0.48-0.3)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.4(0.6)}{100}+\dfrac{0.4(0.6)}{80}}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
A political consultant wondered if support for a candidate was significantly different between voters who owned a landline telephone and voters who did not. The consultant surveyed a random sample of voters and found $36$ of $75$ who owned a landline supported the candidate, while $20$ of $100$ who did not own a landline supported the candidate.

The consultant wants to test if these results suggest a significant difference in support between voters who do and do not own a landline. Assume that all conditions have been met.

**Which of the following would be an appropriate test statistic for their test?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
###Pooling the proportion of successes

In this type of test, it's useful to first calculate the pooled (or combined) proportion of successes in both samples:

$\hat p_\text{C}=\dfrac{\text{combined count of successes}}{\text{combined sample sizes}}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Formula for test statistic

The test statistic for this test is:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\text{sample difference}-\text{hypothesized difference}}{\text{standard error of the difference}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\left(\hat p_1-\hat p_2\right)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\hat p_\text{C}(1-\hat p_\text{C})}{n_1}+\dfrac{\hat p_\text{C}(1-\hat p_\text{C})}{n_2}}}
\end{align}$

The consultant surveyed a random sample of voters and found $\blueD{36}$ of $\purpleD{75}$ who owned a landline supported the candidate, while $\greenD{20}$ of $\goldD{100}$ who did not own a landline supported the candidate.

$\begin{align}
\hat p_\text{C}&=\dfrac{\blueD{36}+\greenD{20}}{\purpleD{75}+\goldD{100}}=0.32 \\\\
\hat p_\text{Landline}&=\dfrac{\blueD{36}}{\purpleD{75}}=0.48 \\\\
\hat p_\text{No landline}&=\dfrac{\greenD{20}}{\goldD{100}}=0.2
\end{align}$

Plugging these values in, our test statistic is:

$z=\dfrac{(0.48-0.2)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.32(0.68)}{75}+\dfrac{0.32(0.68)}{100}}}$

###Answer

An appropriate test statistic would be:

$z=\dfrac{(0.48-0.2)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.32(0.68)}{75}+\dfrac{0.32(0.68)}{100}}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Researchers suspect that myopia, or nearsightedness, is becoming more common over time. A study from the year $2000$ showed $132$ cases of myopia in $400$ randomly selected people. A separate study from $2015$ showed $228$ cases in $600$ randomly selected people. 

The researchers want to use these results to test $H_0\text{: }p_{2015}-p_{2000}=0$ versus $H_\text{a}\text{: }p_{2015}-p_{2000}>0$. Assume that all conditions have been met.

**Which of the following would be an appropriate test statistic for their test?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Pooling the proportion of successes

In this type of test, it's useful to first calculate the pooled (or combined) proportion of successes in both samples:

$\hat p_\text{C}=\dfrac{\text{combined count of successes}}{\text{combined sample sizes}}$

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###Formula for test statistic

The test statistic for this test is:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\text{sample difference}-\text{hypothesized difference}}{\text{standard error of the difference}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\left(\hat p_1-\hat p_2\right)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\hat p_\text{C}(1-\hat p_\text{C})}{n_1}+\dfrac{\hat p_\text{C}(1-\hat p_\text{C})}{n_2}}}
\end{align}$

###Components of formula

A study from the year $2000$ showed $\greenD{132}$ cases of myopia in $\goldD{400}$ randomly selected people. A separate study from $2015$ showed $\blueD{228}$ cases in $\purpleD{600}$ randomly selected people.

$\begin{align}
\hat p_\text{C}&=\dfrac{\blueD{228}+\greenD{132}}{\purpleD{600}+\goldD{400}}=0.36 \\\\
\hat p_\text{2015}&=\dfrac{\blueD{228}}{\purpleD{600}}=0.38 \\\\
\hat p_\text{2000}&=\dfrac{\greenD{132}}{\goldD{400}}=0.33
\end{align}$

The alternative hypothesis is $H_\text{a}\text{: }p_{2015}-p_{2000}>0$, so our sample difference should follow that order as well $(\hat p_{2015}-\hat p_{2000})$.

Plugging these values in, our test statistic is:

$z=\dfrac{(0.38-0.33)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.36(0.64)}{600}+\dfrac{0.36(0.64)}{400}}}$

###Answer

An appropriate test statistic would be:

$z=\dfrac{(0.38-0.33)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.36(0.64)}{600}+\dfrac{0.36(0.64)}{400}}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
An economist was curious if women were more satisfied with their jobs than men. A random sample of $220$ workers showed that $46$ of $100$ women were satisfied with their jobs, and $42$ of $120$ men were satisfied.

The economist wants to use these results to test $H_0\text{: }p_\text{W}-p_\text{M}=0$ versus $H_\text{a}\text{: }p_\text{W}-p_\text{M}>0$. Assume that all conditions have been met.

**Which of the following would be an appropriate test statistic for their test?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Pooling the proportion of successes

In this type of test, it's useful to first calculate the pooled (or combined) proportion of successes in both samples:

$\hat p_\text{C}=\dfrac{\text{combined count of successes}}{\text{combined sample sizes}}$

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###Formula for test statistic

The test statistic for this test is:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\text{sample difference}-\text{hypothesized difference}}{\text{standard error of the difference}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\left(\hat p_1-\hat p_2\right)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\hat p_\text{C}(1-\hat p_\text{C})}{n_1}+\dfrac{\hat p_\text{C}(1-\hat p_\text{C})}{n_2}}}
\end{align}$

###Components of formula

A random sample of $220$ workers showed that $\blueD{46}$ of $\purpleD{100}$ women were satisfied with their jobs, and $\greenD{42}$ of $\goldD{120}$ men were satisfied.

$\begin{align}
\hat p_\text{C}&=\dfrac{\blueD{46}+\greenD{42}}{\purpleD{100}+\goldD{120}}=0.4 \\\\
\hat p_\text{W}&=\dfrac{\blueD{46}}{\purpleD{100}}=0.46 \\\\
\hat p_\text{M}&=\dfrac{\greenD{42}}{\goldD{120}}=0.35
\end{align}$

The alternative hypothesis is $H_\text{a}\text{: }p_\text{W}-p_\text{M}>0$, so our sample difference should follow that order as well $(\hat p_\text{W}-\hat p_\text{M})$.

Plugging these values in, our test statistic is:

$z=\dfrac{(0.46-0.35)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.4(0.6)}{100}+\dfrac{0.4(0.6)}{120}}}$

###Answer

An appropriate test statistic would be:

$z=\dfrac{(0.46-0.35)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.4(0.6)}{100}+\dfrac{0.4(0.6)}{120}}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
An economist was comparing unemployment rates between states. A random sample of $1000$ adults in Alaska showed that $70$ were unemployed, and a random sample of  $1000$ adults in Minnesota showed that $30$ were unemployed.

The economist want to use these results to test $H_0\text{: }p_\text{AK}-p_\text{MN}=0$ versus $H_\text{a}\text{: }p_\text{AK}-p_\text{MN}>0$. Assume that all conditions have been met.

**Which of the following would be an appropriate test statistic for their test?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Pooling the proportion of successes

In this type of test, it's useful to first calculate the pooled (or combined) proportion of successes in both samples:

$\hat p_\text{C}=\dfrac{\text{combined count of successes}}{\text{combined sample sizes}}$

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###Formula for test statistic

The test statistic for this test is:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\text{sample difference}-\text{hypothesized difference}}{\text{standard error of the difference}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\left(\hat p_1-\hat p_2\right)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\hat p_\text{C}(1-\hat p_\text{C})}{n_1}+\dfrac{\hat p_\text{C}(1-\hat p_\text{C})}{n_2}}}
\end{align}$

###Components of formula

A random sample of $\purpleD{1000}$ adults in Alaska showed that $\blueD{70}$ were unemployed, and a random sample of  $\goldD{1000}$ adults in Minnesota showed that $\greenD{30}$ were unemployed.

$\begin{align}
\hat p_\text{C}&=\dfrac{\blueD{70}+\greenD{30}}{\purpleD{1000}+\goldD{1000}}=0.05 \\\\
\hat p_\text{AK}&=\dfrac{\blueD{70}}{\purpleD{1000}}=0.07 \\\\
\hat p_\text{MN}&=\dfrac{\greenD{30}}{\goldD{1000}}=0.03
\end{align}$

The alternative hypothesis is $H_\text{a}\text{: }p_\text{AK}-p_\text{MN}>0$, so our sample difference should follow that order as well $(\hat p_\text{AK}-\hat p_\text{MN})$.

Plugging these values in, our test statistic is:

$z=\dfrac{(0.07-0.03)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.05(0.95)}{1000}+\dfrac{0.05(0.95)}{1000}}}$

###Answer

An appropriate test statistic would be:

$z=\dfrac{(0.07-0.03)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.05(0.95)}{1000}+\dfrac{0.05(0.95)}{1000}}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Researchers studying osteoporosis (bone loss) suspected that women over the age of $50$ in the United States are diagnosed with the disease more often than women over $50$ in Mexico. They took a random sample of $200$ women over the age of $50$ from each country. Here are the results:

Has osteoporosis? | US | Mexico
- | -: | -:
Yes | $40$ | $20$
No | $160$ | $180$
**Total** | $200$ | $200$

The researchers want to use these results to test $H_0\text{: }p_\text{US}-p_\text{M}=0$ versus $H_\text{a}\text{: }p_\text{US}-p_\text{M}>0$. Assume that all conditions have been met.

**Which of the following would be an appropriate test statistic for their test?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Pooling the proportion of successes

In this type of test, it's useful to first calculate the pooled (or combined) proportion of successes in both samples:

$\hat p_\text{C}=\dfrac{\text{combined count of successes}}{\text{combined sample sizes}}$

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###Formula for test statistic

The test statistic for this test is:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\text{sample difference}-\text{hypothesized difference}}{\text{standard error of the difference}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\left(\hat p_1-\hat p_2\right)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\hat p_\text{C}(1-\hat p_\text{C})}{n_1}+\dfrac{\hat p_\text{C}(1-\hat p_\text{C})}{n_2}}}
\end{align}$

###Components of formula

Has osteoporosis? | US | Mexico
- | -: | -:
Yes | $\blueD{40}$ | $\greenD{20}$
No | $160$ | $180$
**Total** | $\purpleD{200}$ | $\goldD{200}$

$\begin{align}
\hat p_\text{C}&=\dfrac{\blueD{40}+\greenD{20}}{\purpleD{200}+\goldD{200}}=0.15 \\\\
\hat p_\text{US}&=\dfrac{\blueD{40}}{\purpleD{200}}=0.2 \\\\
\hat p_\text{M}&=\dfrac{\greenD{20}}{\goldD{200}}=0.1
\end{align}$

The alternative hypothesis is $H_\text{a}\text{: }p_\text{US}-p_\text{M}>0$, so our sample difference should follow that order as well $(\hat p_\text{US}-\hat p_\text{M})$.

Plugging these values in, our test statistic is:

$z=\dfrac{(0.2-0.1)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.15(0.85)}{200}+\dfrac{0.15(0.85)}{200}}}$

###Answer

An appropriate test statistic would be:

$z=\dfrac{(0.2-0.1)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.15(0.85)}{200}+\dfrac{0.15(0.85)}{200}}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
An egg supplier sources its eggs from two farms—farm A and farm B. The supplier suspects that a larger proportion of eggs from farm A have dangerous amounts of salmonella. The supplier took a random sample of $1000$ eggs from each farm. Here are the results:

Salmonella? | Farm A | Farm B
- | -: | -:
Dangerous | $50$ | $10$
Not | $950$ | $990$
**Total** | $1000$ | $1000$

The supplier wants to use these results to test $H_0\text{: }p_\text{A}-p_\text{B}=0$ versus $H_\text{a}\text{: }p_\text{A}-p_\text{B}>0$. Assume that all conditions have been met.

**Which of the following would be an appropriate test statistic for their test?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Pooling the proportion of successes

In this type of test, it's useful to first calculate the pooled (or combined) proportion of successes in both samples:

$\hat p_\text{C}=\dfrac{\text{combined count of successes}}{\text{combined sample sizes}}$

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###Formula for test statistic

The test statistic for this test is:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\text{sample difference}-\text{hypothesized difference}}{\text{standard error of the difference}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\left(\hat p_1-\hat p_2\right)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\hat p_\text{C}(1-\hat p_\text{C})}{n_1}+\dfrac{\hat p_\text{C}(1-\hat p_\text{C})}{n_2}}}
\end{align}$

###Components of formula

Salmonella? | Farm A | Farm B
- | -: | -:
Dangerous | $\blueD{50}$ | $\greenD{10}$
Not | $950$ | $990$
**Total** | $\purpleD{1000}$ | $\goldD{1000}$

$\begin{align}
\hat p_\text{C}&=\dfrac{\blueD{50}+\greenD{10}}{\purpleD{1000}+\goldD{1000}}=0.03 \\\\
\hat p_\text{A}&=\dfrac{\blueD{50}}{\purpleD{1000}}=0.05 \\\\
\hat p_\text{B}&=\dfrac{\greenD{10}}{\goldD{1000}}=0.01
\end{align}$

The alternative hypothesis is $H_\text{a}\text{: }p_\text{A}-p_\text{B}>0$, so our sample difference should follow that order as well $(\hat p_\text{A}-\hat p_\text{B})$.

Plugging these values in, our test statistic is:

$z=\dfrac{(0.05-0.01)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.03(0.97)}{1000}+\dfrac{0.03(0.97)}{1000}}}$

###Answer

An appropriate test statistic would be:

$z=\dfrac{(0.05-0.01)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.03(0.97)}{1000}+\dfrac{0.03(0.97)}{1000}}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Caroline thinks she can flip a coin so it lands showing heads more often with her right hand. She flipped a coin $40$ times with each hand. Here are the results:

Result | Right | Left
- | -: | -:
Heads | $26$ | $18$
Tails | $14$ | $22$
**Total** | $40$ | $40$

Caroline wants to use these results to test $H_0\text{: }p_\text{R}-p_\text{L}=0$ versus $H_\text{a}\text{: }p_\text{R}-p_\text{L}>0$. Assume that all conditions have been met.

**Which of the following would be an appropriate test statistic for her test?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
###Pooling the proportion of successes

In this type of test, it's useful to first calculate the pooled (or combined) proportion of successes in both samples:

$\hat p_\text{C}=\dfrac{\text{combined count of successes}}{\text{combined sample sizes}}$

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###Formula for test statistic

The test statistic for this test is:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\text{sample difference}-\text{hypothesized difference}}{\text{standard error of the difference}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\left(\hat p_1-\hat p_2\right)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\hat p_\text{C}(1-\hat p_\text{C})}{n_1}+\dfrac{\hat p_\text{C}(1-\hat p_\text{C})}{n_2}}}
\end{align}$

###Components of formula

Result | Right | Left
- | -: | -:
Heads | $\blueD{26}$ | $\greenD{18}$
Tails | $14$ | $22$
**Total** | $\purpleD{40}$ | $\goldD{40}$

$\begin{align}
\hat p_\text{C}&=\dfrac{\blueD{26}+\greenD{18}}{\purpleD{40}+\goldD{40}}=0.55 \\\\
\hat p_\text{R}&=\dfrac{\blueD{26}}{\purpleD{40}}=0.65 \\\\
\hat p_\text{L}&=\dfrac{\greenD{18}}{\goldD{40}}=0.45
\end{align}$

The alternative hypothesis is $H_\text{a}\text{: }p_\text{R}-p_\text{L}>0$, so our sample difference should follow that order as well $(\hat p_\text{R}-\hat p_\text{L})$.

Plugging these values in, our test statistic is:

$z=\dfrac{(0.65-0.45)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.55(0.45)}{40}+\dfrac{0.55(0.45)}{40}}}$

###Answer

An appropriate test statistic would be:

$z=\dfrac{(0.65-0.45)-0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.55(0.45)}{40}+\dfrac{0.55(0.45)}{40}}}$
